sentences.text: Terrible.
I was expecting every day that they'd come and fetch me and the children.
But I don't know.
They didn't come after me.
And it was about a year.
And of course, I know what they've done with my husband.
And I tried everything to get him out.
I sold-- sold all my jewelry.
And of course, I was a target for being ripped off.
And the people-- I got messages.
If I gave so and so much jewelry to that and that person, they would give it to the Nazis, and they would release my husband, and let him out.
But of course, I sold the jewelry.
I gave it to this thief.
And of course, that was the end.
And nobody ever-- my husband never came back.
And he was just sent to Auschwitz.
And-- at least that's what I was told.

Well, I don't think-- you know, when one sort of talked to-- I can't remember exactly who said it was known like that, or said, that he's probably gone to Auschwitz, you know, more in that conversation.
Because I never wanted to get to-- I had to protect my children.
I was their-- it was all that was left to them.
There's me.
And I was English, so with this awful accent that I couldn't rid myself of, this English accent.
Well, it was, of course.
Because-- well, you know, that the French, when they speak English with that French accent, people immediately say, oh, you're French.
Aren't-- don't they?
You know ze, [INAUDIBLE].
They can't say the 'the' and all that, the French.
Yes, but of course, I bucked up.
And I thought, I must do something about it.
So my accent-- I just sort of tried to lose that English accent, the R's.
And I used to use words that didn't have an R, so I didn't get caught with it.
Yes.
Oh, when I was married?
No, I had to give up my English passport.
It was awful.
But I did go and get it-- get a double nationality.
I had double nationality, English and Dutch, somehow or other.
Yes, many times.
But I got-- you know, I was in this lovely house.
And-- and the children-- well, they didn't go to school.
Because they weren't allowed to go to-- they could only go to a Jewish school, which was run by people-- every time, somebody new came.
Because the poor teachers were always arrested and taken off to Auschwitz.
And so they had to get new people.
It was really no-- they didn't really learn anything much there, at that school.
It was pathetic.
And then-- what happened?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Oh, yes.

Some very good friends of my husband-- non-Jewish people-- there was some arrangement with the Germans that Jews who had done a lot for social work-- not only Jewish work, but you know, for the community-- could have special protection if they've done a lot for Holland.
And my husband was put on that list.
Because he had done an awful lot of social work and, as I told you, giving all this free advice and looking after-- oh, he had a lot of social things that he used to-- hockey.
He had hockey clubs, and what all things-- a lot of things that he did for-- oh, to make people's lives pleasant.
And so people you know, were horrified to hear that he'd been arrested and vanished.
So on the one hand, you got put onto a list--
Yes.
--to get special kind of protection because of him.
Yes, so I wasn't supposed to be deported from Holland.
That was what it was.
I was on this list that I was allowed-- I was allowed to stay in Holland.
I don't-- under what-- I didn't have to go to Auschwitz.
Yes.
But it was too late, you see, for him.
This all happened right at the beginning of the war that they arrested him.
And I managed to hang on for, I think, about another year and a half, til one morning-- oh, yes, that's what it was.
They had this castle, an old castle.
It was not a proper castle.
It was a broken-down, old, big, enormous house.
And these people that were on this list were all put in this castle.
And we had to run the castle ourselves-- cook, do all the laundry and everything.
But we weren't deported.
And it was right out, miles away from everywhere.
Yes, it was in Holland.
And so we were allowed-- in the beginning, we were allowed visitors.
You know, our-- but the people that came to visit us were very brave.
Because they were then tainted with the same brush.
You know, well, these are Jew friends, and all that horrible thing.
So some people, of course, who had children, didn't want to take any risks, which we all understood that.
So there weren't so many visits from on the outside world as in the beginning.
And then one morning, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they came.
And they said, pack up a knapsack.
We're taking you to-- they didn't say where, but to a-- to the train.
And we had to be ready in an hour.
And that's what I had to do-- get all these children's knapsacks ready, my knapsack packed-- just the necessaries.
And we were all packed into a lorry, and driven off to a train in Amsterdam, and locked in carriages.
And then, after long, long hours, we arrived in the dark.
It was in Germany that they'd got us to.
And--
Yes, that's right.
Wait a minute.
When did I go to Westerbork?
Yes, it was this castle.
You're right!
But I've got in a muddle.
I've missed out the whole of Westerbork.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I really can't remember it.
Oh, yes.
These are all the very important people in Holland of Jewish origins.
You put it like that-- judges, lawyers, doctors, sort of that [FRENCH]-- schoolmasters.
Not very long.
I think about six months or something like that.
It wasn't a bit nice.
Because I'd been very spoilt and had a nanny for my children.
You know, I was used to the English life.
And then I suddenly was landed with three very naughty little boys.
And I'm not a bit of an authoritative person.
Nobody listened to me when I said, you mustn't do that.
[LAUGHS] So I had a pretty hard life ruling my own children, which are the real little devils, they were.
Oh, she was arrested right in the beginning, when they were deported.
They came one-- one night and took her out of her house.
Well, I heard that.
You see, we weren't allowed to travel.
So I couldn't go and see her.
I just was told by somebody else, they've come and arrested your mother and taken her away.
And I-- I heard no more from her, ever.
Oh, my eldest sister was also taken with her husband.
She lived in another part of Holland, where they went and arrested Jews.
And-- oh, usually in the night, or in the early morning, about 2 o'clock in the morning, 3 o'clock, when everybody was asleep, they came and knocked on the door and forced the door in.
They knew who they were after.
And then that was the end.
Nobody heard any more from--
Mm.
Well, she was in America.
She married an American and escaped to America.
I don't think she knew, though, then.
She just was-- her husband was American.
So she went there.
So she didn't know anything what was happening at all.
Yes.
I had to take all the decisions about what I-- what I was going to do.
And, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] yes.
But the decisions that I-- a big decision was when I was-- I've got in a muddle with this last bit.
I can't seem to remember it properly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After-- after the castle, they arrested us.
And they sent us-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes, I think we must have first gone to Westerbork, before we went-- that's right.
We were a time in Westerbork, in that camp.
That was a sort of Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], sort of-- what do you call it, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where they collected you and-- before they sent you on.
I think it was a transit, a transit camp.
A transit camp.
That's right.
And from there, from Westerbork, we were sent in that train to-- to Germany.
But where did they send us?
Oh, god, I--
That's it.
Yes.
I didn't go-- I went-- that was supposed to be-- we weren't supposed to be murdered.
We were supposed to be allowed to be in Theresienstadt and allowed to live.
We weren't allowed to be in Holland anymore.
Oh, yes.
I know Westerbork very well.
Well-- well, I was a young woman there.
And there was a young man that worked in the kitchen, who rather fancied me.
And I thought, so what?
I haven't got my husband.
He would understand.
And so he provided me with food.
And I let him make love to me [LAUGHS] to put it crudely, which wasn't unpleasant.
He was a nice chap.
He was miserable, too.
But he worked in the kitchens.
So he always brought food for my children.
And that sort of helped them to survive on the rotten bits that we got to eat.
I never felt any guilt at all about it.
I thought it was a rather sensible thing to do.
Because then nobody was miserable.
I didn't feel I was being unfaithful to my husband at all.
Of course, when I tell it to you, who live in the-- a different world, goodness knows what you must think of me.
But there we are.
That's the way it was.
And that went on for some time, those few months there.
And then the poor chap was sent to Auschwitz.
And that was the end of him.
And at that time, then we were sent off to Theresienstadt.
Not much.
I wasn't there terribly long.
Because it was only this sort of-- any minute we were sort of waiting to go on to the next horrible stage.
Yes.
I was angry.
And I thought, they're not going to get me down, because that's exactly what they want.
They want to get me right down.
And I'm not going to go.
Because I was the only one that was-- had to keep my brains together, and make the decisions, and not collapse in a state of misery, and get all emotional about how awful everything was.
I had to keep sane.
That was the thing I had to do.
I couldn't indulge in a bit of weeping and giving in to saying how awful everything was.
And I couldn't go-- I did have one period that I thought, it's all got too much for me.
I can't-- I can't cope with any more.
I give in.
And then I suddenly managed to pull myself together and say, no, you're not going to.
You've got to hang on.
So I did.
Yes.
Very naughty, but very adorable.
And of course, I was the only thing that was strong in their lives.
And I used to tell them, don't worry about anything.
As long as I'm with you, nothing will happen to you.
The Germans can't do anything to you.
I'm here.
And that's all that you need worry about.
And-- and they believed me, thank goodness, for a bit.
But then gradually, as the years went on, they grew older.
And all these things that have happened to them-- and 'where's father,' and all that, you know-- all the horrible-- they had questions.
These are the things you couldn't answer.
You just can't say, these are wicked people.
You mustn't talk about it.
Otherwise, we'll all get shot.
So they had to learn to be silent, which is a hard thing for little children.
But they're pretty good kids.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I tried to make it as normal as possible, their lives.
You know, I used to make them toys out of bits of string or try and get a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well-- they-- I don't know how they really came out so well.
Because none of them have got any phobias.
And I never taught them to hate the Germans.
Because I didn't believe that that was a good thing, to hate.
That-- you don't achieve anything by hatred.
You only get things more and more in a muddle.
So you-- you don't have to love them.
But you can ignore them.
It's about the best you can do without upsetting your own emotions.
And children can-- you know, they get something that they're all together with other children, and they make their own lives, and their own games.
Well, the little one was, of course, only about four when-- and the others were six and nine.
So they were in a different group, for older children.
Yes, there were other children, young kids, the same age as [?
Carol, ?] who was then four.
But gradually he was five.
And then of course, when he first went in-- big tears, because he was only five.
And the five-year-olds weren't allowed to wear a-- didn't have to wear the star.
And the others, who were older-- they were made to wear the star.
But [?
Carol, ?] because he didn't get the star and the others did, considered it a great insult, and began crying, because he didn't get the star to wear.
He had to wait a whole year before he was allowed to wear the star, which I made it, as if it was a great honor, you see, not as an insult.
But it amused me that he wept because he couldn't wear this star, which was supposed to be such a great-- [LAUGHS] --insult to wear it, the Star of David, poor old David.
Yes, yes.
It was not too bad in Holland.
Because it was Holland.
You-- once you left the country, you-- god knows what was going to happen to you.
You know, that's the feeling I had.
And I didn't want to find out about England, and, oh, god, and-- I just-- it was awful.
But then the English won.
And when the war was over, and we heard it, I thought, I'm not staying here to be either bumped off or goodness knows what.
I'm getting out with my three kids.
Oh, yes, Theresienstadt.
We went to Theresienstadt.
Yes, in the cattle trucks.
That's right.
Yes, it was very difficult.
Because of course, your bodily functions, if you wanted to go to the loo, it had to be in front of everybody else.
It wasn't-- pretty awful, when you're grown up.
And well, we got through all that, you know, in the end.
If we're all brought to the level of everybody the same, you get over your-- your inhibitions.
You have to get your dignity from somewhere else.
[LAUGHS]
Well I suppose I, in a way, believed in god.
But I thought he did play me a dirty trick by getting me into this muddle.
And gradually, the more difficulties I got into, the less religious I became.
And in the end, I really didn't believe in God at all like that.

I sort of clung onto god because it was the only thing that was sort of left to me from-- I used to sort of say to myself, you're imagining that there's this great spirit up there that's going to help you.
But you might as well appeal to him.
Because there's nothing else to appeal to.
So I sort of jogged along in a funny sort of religious way.
Been there ever since, I would say.
Yes, about two years.
Oh, I can't seem to remember it very well at the moment.
I got there and-- oh, well, I had to find a-- I've always had to find a place for everybody, for my kids.
And I was always pretty good at making a home from nothing.
You know, our old blankets-- I always managed to take blankets with me, and made [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some warmth, and-- or rolled up something to make a pillow.
I'm pretty inventive with nothing.
And that's what Theresienstadt seemed to be, in the beginning.
To get some food was always a-- ah, yes, the food.
Now what happened there?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I really can't remember.
I think I might have found a boyfriend there that provided me with food.
Did-- have I told you that?
I can't remember it even.
No.
Probably don't want to remember it.
Yes, we did.
It was such a swindle.
We were given money.
They said, you can go out and buy your food.
But nobody had any food.
So we had all this money.
And as you've seen, I've kept it.
Because I had-- always had a great feeling for history.
I thought, if I get through this, god, I'm going to be able to tell my grandchildren the exciting adventure I had.
I considered it sort of an adventure, you see.
It was an adventure when you'd lived such a quiet, protected life to be suddenly faced with all these real things, real life, life and death.
I mean, death?
What death did we know?
Just ordinary dying after doctor's treatment, but not this-- this death, unknown death.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where did I get to?
We were talking about food and money in Theresienstadt.
Oh, yes, food.
Food was, of course, the great thing to keep-- keep them alive, the children.
I must have--
Yes, one room, with goodness knows how many people lying on the floor, with these blankets.
And I think somehow or other-- wait a minute.
I-- I was pretty handy.
I seem to remember that I got a room to myself, with my kids.
And I didn't have to sleep with all the others.
But how did I do that?
I probably exchanged something for food.
Oh, yes, I think I got a job in the kitchens.
Because I thought, if I get in the kitchens, them I'm near the food at least, even if I don't get it to eat.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes, funny how it's all so vague now.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't remember it.
Yes, I hated it.
I hated-- I never was a communal person and-- at all.
I didn't like people ever much.
And there, to be stuck there, with never a moment's privacy was for me absolute hell.
You know, and you never slept alone.
Always there was somebody snoring next to you, or getting up to go to the loo, or getting up at about 4:00 in the morning.
I used to have to get up very early and char.
I always-- always went in for charring.
Because that was rather basic.
And you got tips for charring, if you charred properly.
They would tip you with a bit of food.
You know, you'd get an extra bit of milk or something.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so somehow or other, I managed to survive.
[LAUGHS]
Yes, there was.
There was.
That was what was so marvelous.
That-- we were there with top of the everything-- of brilliant professors.
And then the Jews are pretty good at organizing themselves.
They organized that there would be lectures, and that professors would give lectures in their specialities.
And you couldn't just go there.
You had to apply for seats.
I went to everything I could get hold of because I--
Well, it was in the evening.
There was-- we were told there'd be a lecturer there and there, somewhere.
So you saw that you organized yourself to be-- to be able to go there.
And we went and listened to-- we got away from the world and into a world of what we were used to.
